body_txt: A truly great Plot Twist is one of the hardest things to pull off in storytelling. Optimally, the writer will want to reveal the twist in such a way that most of the audience is caught off-guard by it, but also make the twist feel satisfying for those viewers who were paying close attention. A major selling point of twists is that they offer two stories for the price of one — The Reveal provides new information which recontextualizes the story . This then provides further enjoyment for those viewers who decide to rewatch the story while looking for all the subtle hints leading up to it ; seeing that the twist was a part of the story's world which the author had been building toward from the very beginning. This page, however, is about when that fails to happen. An Ass Pull is a moment at which the writers pull a narrative development out of thin air in a less-than-graceful manner. This development violates The Law of Conservation of Detail because it drops a plot-critical piece of information without sufficient Foreshadowing or prior justification . It might even contradict information which had already been given earlier, and/or cause prior scenes to no longer make sense when viewed with the twist in mind . This is by far the quickest way to outright shatter your audience's Willing Suspension of Disbelief ( Contrived Coincidences are a close second), especially if it happens later in the story and/or if it is used to resolve the characters' problems rather than create new ones . Strange contrivances that set conflict in motion generally won't break a viewer's suspension of disbelief so long as they happen early in the story. But the further into a narrative that an Ass Pull or Contrived Coincidence is used, the more likely viewers are to cry foul and have their immersion ruined. The reason for this is simple: audiences want to become immersed in fictional worlds and empathize with the characters; they don't want to be thinking about the writer . Audiences can only become immersed in stories insofar as they don't notice the hand of the author—so long as the events feel justified within the context of that story's universe , viewers can pretend that the author doesn't exist and thus buy into the illusion that said universe is real . What this means, then, is that the more time a viewer spends engaging with and becoming emotionally invested in a story, the less they will tolerate it when someone—i.e., the writer— disrupts that story . This is why strange or unlikely contrivances are permissible if they happen early on: the opening chapters of any story are often when the characters and setting are introduced, therefore grabbing viewers' attention. The middle chapters are then focused on developing the characters as they go about their journey, thus heightening emotional investment. After that, the climax will mostly be about resolving each and every struggle which the characters have been dealing with—the viewers' emotional investment is likely going to be at its apex. Thus, if an important detail or plot point is introduced late, it feels as if the writer is forcing themselves into the story's world at the last minute , thus shattering the illusion that the fictional world is real. And the more emotionally invested the audience was at that point, the more frustrated they will be when a writer does this. In cases where a character suddenly gets a new skill without explanation , it's usually explained away as a Chekhov's Classroom or Chekhov's Skill , except the audience never saw the character attending the lecture in question, or any prior examples of them using, or even training that skill. An Ass Pull doesn't necessarily have to resolve or derail a situation, though—many times, an Ass Pull is just used without any greater plot implication and Played for Laughs . Alternatively, they could come up so as to prevent your characters using a Mundane Solution and shortening your 20 minute episode into 20 seconds. Please limit examples on this page to ones that don't fit in either of the other two. This is not to say, however, that you cannot surprise your audience once in a while; especially if your work is in danger of becoming too predictable, boring, and stale . Sometimes audiences actually like having random, nonsensical things happen if it is amusing enough. note Especially if the work is a comedy that doesn't take itself very seriously to begin with. But still, don't push it. But it should be done so sparingly and carefully. There's a difference between occasionally spicing things up here and there to keep the work interesting versus doing crazy, confusing things to the point of becoming an Ass Pull. And an audience that gets too confused can become an annoyed audience that loses interest or even dislikes the work outright. Generally, the problem with an Ass Pull is that it makes it hard to get invested in the work: if the writer is willing to ignore their prior setup, then it creates the sense that nothing in the story actually matters. The term is also used to describe something that the characters make up on the spot . See Writing by the Seat of Your Pants . Sometimes called Sulu's Foil , since it's the opposite of Chekhov's Gun . In Russia , it's called "grand piano in the bushes". Spanish-speaking fandoms refer to it as "Guionazo" or "Poder del Guión" (Power of the Script). Pulling a useful object out of seemingly nowhere is related to Hammerspace . A particularly bad Ass Pull can even produce a Plot Hole or two, and can also create Voodoo Sharks as well. As much as it sounds like it, this trope is not the reverse of Ass Shove (when a character literally pulls an object out of someone's rear, or the opposite). At the opposite end of an Ass Pull is a Captain Obvious Reveal , where a plot twist is considered bad because it is too easy to figure out beforehand, most commonly because there are too many hints leading up to it. Indeed, an Ass Pull can and does happen because the author is trying too hard to avoid a Captain Obvious Reveal.
